title: Taylors Wines appoints new general manager of sales following strategy pivot
summary:
Taylors Wines has today announced the appointment of Mikaela Noonan as general manager of sales for Australia, “marking a significant step in the company’s pivot toward a more focused, premium-led growth strategy”. The winery said that as the Australian wine industry navigates challenging times, it is prioritising premiumisation, reducing complexity, and investing in the long-term health and value of its brands. Noonan joins from Moët Hennesy Australia, where she currently serves as national sales manager – head of independents. Her career experience spans more than 15 years across retail, national accounts, and channel leadership, “underpinned by strong commercial acumen and a deep understanding of both supplier and customer dynamics”.

The post Taylors Wines appoints new general manager of sales following strategy pivot appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
Mikaela Noonan, the new general manager of sales Australia at Taylors Wines. Image supplied

Taylors Wines has today announced the appointment of Mikaela Noonan as general manager of sales for Australia, “marking a significant step in the company’s pivot toward a more focused, premium-led growth strategy”.

The winery said that as the Australian wine industry navigates challenging times, it is prioritising premiumisation, reducing complexity, and investing in the long-term health and value of its brands.

Noonan joins from Moët Hennesy Australia, where she currently serves as national sales manager – head of independents. Her career experience spans more than 15 years across retail, national accounts, and channel leadership, “underpinned by strong commercial acumen and a deep understanding of both supplier and customer dynamics”.

Noonan said that the decision to join Taylors Wines was driven by its clear ambition and the opportunity ahead.

“This is a business with a strong foundation and a clear sense of where it wants to play. The opportunity to help shape a more focused, premium-led growth agenda, while working with a team committed to doing fewer things better, was a compelling one. I’m excited to be part of this next chapter,” she said.

Taylors Wines CEO John Freeman said Noonan’s appointment comes at a pivotal time for the business.

“Mikaela brings strong strategic clarity, commercial discipline, and a deep understanding of the needs and priorities of our customers. She has consistently demonstrated an ability to unlock growth in premium portfolios by focusing on the right opportunities and executing with precision. That is exactly what our business needs as we reset the business for its next phase,” he said.

Freeman emphasised that while industry conditions remain challenging, Taylors Wines is “clear-eyed about where growth will come from”.

“There is no question that the industry is facing pressure. But within that, there are still meaningful pockets of growth and profitability. Our focus is on simplifying where it matters, reducing unnecessary complexity, and backing the strength of our brands where we see the greatest opportunity for sustainable growth.

“We are confident that a more considered and cohesive approach will position us well for the future,” Freeman concluded.

The post Taylors Wines appoints new general manager of sales following strategy pivot appeared first on Winetitles.

title: Hirsch Wine Group launches South American inspired brand featuring ‘lesser-known’ varietals
summary:
The Hirsch Wine Group has launched its new brand of South American inspired ‘lesser-known’ varietals La Chica Con Tina to high praise, with wine critic duo Master of Wine Meg Brodtmann and Mel Gilcrist ranking it in their Top 10 wines of 2025. Translated from Spanish as ‘The Girl With Tattoos’, the brand features two unique varietals – a “vibrant” Arinto and a medium-bodied Garnacha. Hirsch Wine Group said that the wines intend to showcase how Old-World grapes can flourish under Australian skies to produce bold and individual styles. The Arinto and Garnacha grapes are crafted in small batches from “carefully selected parcels” in Heathcote, a region in Victoria known for its ancient soils and generous sunshine

The post Hirsch Wine Group launches South American inspired brand featuring ‘lesser-known’ varietals appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
La Chica Con Tina includes an Arinto and a Garnacha. Image supplied

The Hirsch Wine Group has launched its new brand of South American inspired ‘lesser-known’ varietals La Chica Con Tina to high praise, with wine critic duo Master of Wine Meg Brodtmann and Mel Gilcrist ranking it in their Top 10 wines of 2025.

Translated from Spanish as ‘The Girl With Tattoos’, the brand features two unique varietals – a “vibrant” Arinto and a medium-bodied Garnacha.

Hirsch Wine Group said that the wines intend to showcase how Old-World grapes can flourish under Australian skies to produce bold and individual styles. The Arinto and Garnacha grapes are crafted in small batches from “carefully selected parcels” in Heathcote, a region in Victoria known for its ancient soils and generous sunshine.

The 2025 Arinto is a Heathcote white which aims to capture the sun-drenched spirit of Portugal “reimagined through Australian soil”. Hirsch Wine Group said the wine contains layers of “bright citrus, juicy melon, and ripe stone fruit” intertwined with “hints of delicate florals and a lift of citrus zest” for a crisp taste. The winery said there are also notes of grapefruit and subtle brioche “over a fine line of acidity, finishing with a textured, refreshing edge”.

The 2025 Garnacha is a Heathcote red with a “delicate savoury edge” layered with “fragrant red berries, strawberry and cherry then lifted by hints of clove, orange rind, and spice. Hirsch Wine Group said that the Spanish inspired red is “delightfully different” and should be served chilled.

The project is said to hold personal significance for Hirsch Wine Group CEO Ben Hirsch and chief winemaker Peter Mackey, who both share “a strong affinity for Spanish and Latin American culture”. Hirsch has lived in Spain before, whilst Mackey has worked as a winemaker in Chile, and both speak Spanish.

title: Riverland soars in new Qantas magazine feature campaign
summary:
The Riverland wine region will be spotlighted in back-to-back editions of the Qantas in-flight magazine, positioning the region in front of domestic and international travellers. Through this new promotion, a monthly audience of up to 2.4 million people from across Australia and the world will be introduced to the vast array of premium wines from “a region defined by big skies, bold ideas and the nation’s greatest river at its heart”. The campaign is a part of a partnership between Riverland Wine and Destination Riverland, backed by almost $25,000 from Project 250 – an industry support fund delivered by the South Australian Wine Industry Association and the state government

The post Riverland soars in new Qantas magazine feature campaign appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
The Riverland will be showcased in back-to-back edition of the Qantas in-flight magazine. Image supplied

The Riverland wine region will be spotlighted in back-to-back editions of the Qantas in-flight magazine, positioning the region in front of domestic and international travellers.

Through this new promotion, a monthly audience of up to 2.4 million people from across Australia and the world will be introduced to the vast array of premium wines from “a region defined by big skies, bold ideas and the nation’s greatest river at its heart”.

The campaign is a part of a partnership between Riverland Wine and Destination Riverland, backed by almost $25,000 from Project 250 – an industry support fund delivered by the South Australian Wine Industry Association and the state government.

Minister for Primary Industries and Regional Development, Clare Scriven MLC, said that the government is investing in SA’s winemakers to ensure they can continue to maintain and build the state’s reputation as “the best place in the world to produce, export, and consume premium wine”.

This month’s edition of the Qantas in-flight magazine features a full-page pictorial advertisement showcasing the region’s iconic Murray River and promoting the region’s wine story as a “distinctive Australian wine destination”.

“People may know us for the Murray River, but what they discover here is a dynamic wine community grounded in family, innovation and a deep connection to the land,” said Lauren Ward, general manager for Destination Riverland.

Brigid Nolan, Riverland Wine Chair, said that the campaign is about rebuilding confidence and creating new demand at a time when it is needed the most.

“The Riverland has always been one of Australia’s most important wine-producing regions. By placing our story in front of a national and international audience, we’re actively working to strengthen our regional brand, support visitation, and open pathways into new markets,” Nolan said.

Preparations are already underway for more complementary advertising in the May issue, which will extend the Riverland’s visibility among high-value visitors and wine-curious customers.

The promotion follows a two-page story in the March edition where Qantas content writer Helen Martin independently attested to the Riverland’s landscape, innovation and evolving wine story.

title: Ag leaders demand better Basin management on the Murray River
summary:
The National Farmers’ Federation (NFF), alongside NSW Farmers and Victorian Farmers’ Federation (VFF) stood together last Friday on the banks of the Murray River with a message for the federal government. They demanded that the government deliver smarter Basin management and rule out policies that would strip more water from food production and regional communities “at the worst possible time”. With multiple major water reviews underway and critical decisions imminent, the agriculture leaders said this is the time to reset the approach with one grounded in evidence, practical delivery, and genuine engagement with the people who live and work in the nation’s food sector

The post Ag leaders demand better Basin management on the Murray River appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
The National Farmers’ Federation (NFF), alongside NSW Farmers and Victorian Farmers’ Federation (VFF) stood together last Friday on the banks of the Murray River with a message for the federal government.

They demanded that the government deliver smarter Basin management and rule out policies that would strip more water from food production and regional communities “at the worst possible time”.

With multiple major water reviews underway and critical decisions imminent, the agriculture leaders said this is the time to reset the approach with one grounded in evidence, practical delivery, and genuine engagement with the people who live and work in the nation’s food sector.

The organisations reinforced a strong opposition to water acquisition in any form, including under the 450GL target, Sustainable Diversion Limit adjustments and through rules-based changes that “effectively strip water from productive use”.

NFF president Hamish McIntyre said that the mistakes of the past must not be repeated.

“Communities are still hurting from years of successive buybacks. We’ve seen the damage when policy is done to regions and not with them, and we won’t stand by and let that happen again.

“Farmers are already under pressure from rising input costs and global uncertainty, including the escalating fuel and fertiliser supply issues. If you take away water on top of that, you’re not just hurting farmers, you’re putting Australia’s food and fibre supply at risk,” he said.

McIntyre acknowledged the calls for a Royal Commission into Basin management; however, he urged careful consideration amidst the frustration.

“With multiple substantive reviews already underway and legislative changes on the horizon, the priority right now must be on getting these reforms right. We can’t afford to lose more time or risk unintended consequences.”

The joint delegation drew attention to the draft findings of the Basin Plan Review that recognises the requirement for “smarter water management approaches and projects to improve water quality and address native fish decline, including carp eradication”.

The leaders called for the following:

  • Practical mechanisms that deliver enhanced environmental outcomes and that simply adding more water won’t translate to environmental improvement.
  • A national fish strategy that focuses on real outcomes that reduce fish kills and eradicate carp numbers.
  • A planned approach to repairing or replacing ageing infrastructure, some of which is a century old.
  • Moving on from ‘tinkering’ with rules and water acquisition by stealth.
  • Actively supporting a viable and prosperous agricultural sector that underpins local communities, sustains jobs and safeguards food production for every Australian.

NSW Farmers president Xavier Martin said the government needs to be careful not to cause further water shortages that then create food shortages.

“We want a Basin Plan that supports agricultural productivity, protects regional communities, ensures transparent and accountable Basin governance, and uses science based environmental management rather than blanket water recovery targets.

“The collapsing rivers, the science, and the evidence say we don’t need more buybacks that pour taxpayer money into the ocean, we need projects that use water wisely,” Martin said.

The organisations said that they are actively engaged across four major processes “shaping the future of water policy” including the Basin Plan Review, the Productivity Commission’s Inquiry into National Water Reform, the review of the Water Act 2007, and the review of the Inspector-General of Water Compliance.

The joint representation believes that these processes “must speak to each other” and produce a coherent, workable path forward – “not more uncertainty”.

VFF president Brett Hoskins said that they expect to be listened to.

“We want a Basin Plan that works in the real world. That means smarter management, proper investment, and policies that back in regional communities and avoids destructive buybacks,” he said.

Are you a Daily Wine News subscriber? If not, click here to join our mailing list. It’s free!

The post Ag leaders demand better Basin management on the Murray River appeared first on Winetitles.

title: Friday Feedback – 10/04
summary:
If you’ve been reading the news and wondering what your peers think of it, look no further. Our Friday Feedback segment provides a platform for the voices of our readers, highlighting the thoughts, reflections and musings of members of the wine industry. See a reader’s response to our last Friday Feedback section and a response to Wine Australia cancelling an export licence last week.

The post Friday Feedback – 10/04 appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
If you’ve been reading the news and wondering what your peers think of it, look no further.

Our Friday Feedback segment provides a platform for the voices of our readers, highlighting the thoughts, reflections and musings of members of the wine industry.

Read our recent roundup here:

 

Responding to Friday Feedback – 27/03

“This EU Trade agreement has a big hidden penalty for all Australians. The “fine print” in this agreement locks Australia into the Paris Agreement and Net Zero. Previously the Paris Agreement and Net Zero was voluntary.

“Also Italian & French wines will be cheaper in Australia, this will hurt particularly our small premium wine producers particularly now when the Australian wine industry is in financial trouble with oversupply, falling consumption and reduced exports.”

  • Ausecco for me too

 

Responding to Wine Australia cancels export licence to ‘protect Australian wine reputation’

“Wine Australia cancels export licence to ‘protect Australian wine reputation’…Protect Australian Wine reputation from what?

“Why was Jindalee’s export license cancelled in the first place?

“Is the current batch of wine CoLab is exporting for Jindalee also breaking the same rules that lost Jindalee’s licence? If Not, what is the problem, is CoLab being targeted by WA for acting as an agent for a banned exporter?

“It’s such a “Nothing, Empty” statement from WA that smacks of high handedness.”

  • CM

 

Read something in Daily Wine News that’s got you thinking? Tell us what you think for the chance to have your comment featured in our Friday Feedback segment. Leave a comment through the button below or contact our editorial team at: journalist@winetitles.com.au.

Are you a Daily Wine News subscriber? If not, click here to join our mailing list. It’s free!

The post Friday Feedback – 10/04 appeared first on Winetitles.

title: TWE marks milestone in China-Australia immersion program with ceremony and roundtable
summary:
Treasury Wine Estates (TWE) last week completed the Australian leg of the 2026 ‘Australia China Winemaker Immersion Program’. The producer hosted participants, industry partners, and government representatives from both nations at Penfolds Magill Estate for a closing ceremony event and a roundtable discussion on Australia-China relations.

The post TWE marks milestone in China-Australia immersion program with ceremony and roundtable appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
L-R: Kou Yaqin, Zheng Wei, Cai Jieling, and Yang Zhe. The four participants of the immersion program. Image supplied

Treasury Wine Estates (TWE) last week completed the Australian leg of the 2026 ‘Australia China Winemaker Immersion Program’.

The producer hosted participants, industry partners, and government representatives from both nations at Penfolds Magill Estate for a closing ceremony event and a roundtable discussion on Australia-China relations.

The ceremony followed an “intensive” three-week exchange program across South Australia’s wine regions where Chinese winemakers Yang Zhe, Kou Yaqin, and Cai Jieling, along with viticulturist Zheng Wei, explored winemaking and viticulture practices and related academic research, gained hands-on experience in winery and vineyard operations during vintage, and examined cellar‑door and tourism activities in Adelaide.

Speaking at the closing ceremony, TWE chief supply and sustainability officer Kerrin Petty said that the winery is proud to once again deliver the program on behalf of the wine industry associations in Australia and China.

“The participants have been immersed in the very best of South Australia’s food, wine, and tourism culture – hosted by our talented and passionate teams at the most exciting time of year in wine: vintage. We wish them well in their future careers and look forward to seeing how their insight into Australian winemaking practice and research helps to shape the future success of the wine industry,” he said.

The guest of honour, Minister Gao Feng, Minister Counsellor for Economic and Commercial Affairs at the Embassy of the People’s Republic of China in Australia, said it was a pleasure to hear about the broad range of industry experiences they’ve had in South Australia.

“Person-to-person and business-to-business connections are an important foundation for strong relationships between governments and economies, so it’s pleasing to see this in practice by Treasury Wine Estates. Congratulations to all the participants for successfully completing the program,” Feng said.

The program is an “industry-first exchange” designed to nurture talent and deepen cultural understanding through immersion into the winemaking practices and market dynamics of both countries. Now in its second year, the program is supported by Australian Grape & Wine (AGW) and the China Alcoholic Drinks Association (CADA).

“The return of the Australia–China Winemaker Immersion Program for a second year demonstrates the value of industry-led collaboration in building capability, trust and long-term relationships. This program is leading the way in strengthening the professional and cultural links that underpin a resilient and sustainable wine sector,” said AGW CEO Lee McLean.

The post TWE marks milestone in China-Australia immersion program with ceremony and roundtable appeared first on Winetitles.

title: Calabria Family Wine Group takes on national distribution of Redman
summary:
Coonawarra winery Redman has revealed that The Calabria Family Wine Group will be its new national distributor as of Monday 13 April 2026. The partnership will see Redman wines return to Western Australia after a 15-year hiatus. Calabria’s acquisition of The Wine Company, who previously distributed Redman across New South Wales, Victoria and Queensland, led to the change.

The post Calabria Family Wine Group takes on national distribution of Redman appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
Calabria will distribute Redman nationally following its acquisition of The Wine Company. Image supplied

Coonawarra winery Redman has revealed that The Calabria Family Wine Group will be its new national distributor as of Monday 13 April 2026.

The partnership will see Redman wines return to Western Australia after a 15-year hiatus.

Calabria’s acquisition of The Wine Company, who previously distributed Redman across New South Wales, Victoria and Queensland, led to the change.

The move has been welcomed by Redman co-owner and fourth-generation winemaker Dan Redman.

“Calabria is a multi-generational, family-owned business like ours, and we are confident in their understanding of our business. We are excited to be a part of their growing distribution company.

“We look forward to continuing to nurture our existing customer relationships, as well as creating many more with Calabria as our new partner,” he said.

Andrew Calabria, head of sales and marketing at Calabria Family Wine Group said that Calabria is very proud to be working with one of Coonawarra’s longest standing family producers.

“The Redman family is at the heart of Australian winemaking, we are excited to represent Redman and look forward to sharing their story and milestones.”

2026 is the 60th year of the Redman brand.

The post Calabria Family Wine Group takes on national distribution of Redman appeared first on Winetitles.

title: Labour law compliance urged as authorities target SA vineyards
summary:
The Australian Taxation Office (ATO), Fair Work Ombudsman (FWO) and Department of Home Affairs’ Australian Border Force (ABF) have conducted joint coordinated unannounced inspections targeting vineyard owners and labour hire providers. Surprise compliance visits to 18 vineyards in the Barossa Valley, Adelaide Hills and McLaren Vale have already been carried out as a part of ‘Operation Zephyr’ under the Shadow Economy Taskforce. Investigators were searching for any unlawful employer practices regarding seasonal vineyard workers, like labour hire providers, including:

The post Labour law compliance urged as authorities target SA vineyards appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
Coordinated unannounced inspections targeted SA vineyard owners and labour hirers. Image supplied

The Australian Taxation Office (ATO), Fair Work Ombudsman (FWO) and Department of Home Affairs’ Australian Border Force (ABF) have conducted joint coordinated unannounced inspections targeting vineyard owners and labour hire providers.

Surprise compliance visits to 18 vineyards in the Barossa Valley, Adelaide Hills and McLaren Vale have already been carried out as a part of ‘Operation Zephyr’ under the Shadow Economy Taskforce.

Officers from all three agencies spoke directly with workers, vineyard managers and labour hire providers and requested detailed records including any labour hire contracts, invoices, timesheets, pay slips and work rosters.

Authorities have indicated that investigations are ongoing.

Australian Grape & Wine (AGW) CEO Lee McLean said that the organisation is aware of Operation Zephyr from media reports.

“We take these allegations very seriously. The Australian wine sector is built on integrity, and compliance with workplace, taxation and migration laws is fundamental to maintaining trust across the industry.

“We note that this is an ongoing investigation and, as reported, no arrests have been made.

“Australian Grape & Wine supports appropriate enforcement action where wrongdoing is identified. Unlawful labour practices and exploitation have no place in our industry and undermine both workers and businesses doing the right thing,” he said.

Inca Lee, chief executive of the South Australian Wine Industry Association (SAWIA), said that it is also aware of the enforcement activity. She said that the association has provided information to its members.

“The South Australian grape and wine industry is committed to operating with integrity and in full compliance with all relevant legislation. Our grape and wine businesses contribute positively to the state’s economy and regional communities.

“SAWIA continues to support members through expert guidance and practical resources to ensure they understand and meet their obligations, and we will continue to work closely with government agencies to maintain high standards across the industry,” she said.

Investigators were searching for “any unlawful practices” by employers of seasonal vineyard workers and labour hire providers, including:

  • underpaying wages, penalty rates and other entitlements including super
  • non-compliance with tax, super and registry obligations
  • failing to withhold and remit Pay as You Go Withholding (PAYGW) tax
  • failing to provide pay slips to workers
  • non-lodgement of activity statements and income tax returns
  • incorrect reporting of business income and expenses
  • breaches of obligations under the Migration Act including migrant worker exploitation and unlawful provision of immigration assistance.

ATO Assistant Commissioner Tony Goding said that unlawful labour practices can cause significant disadvantage to honest operators in the viticulture industry.

“Paying workers off the books, ignoring ATO obligations or using dodgy labour hire providers doesn’t just break the law, it erodes trust in the viticulture industry which employs thousands of people across Australia.

“Our work in South Australia’s grape-growing regions shows we are serious about crushing illegal labour hire practices to ensure a fair go for honest providers,” he said.

Fair Work Ombudsman Anna Booth said that the pay and conditions of the employees on the ground in South Australia’s wine regions need to meet the same high standards as the impressive produce.

“The agriculture sector is a priority for the Fair Work Ombudsman and viticulture is an important part of that focus. We know there are a large number of vulnerable workers employed in grape-picking, packing, and pruning vineyards. Not paying workers their entitlements cannot be part of any grower’s business model.

“Migrant workers must remember that they have the same workplace rights under the Fair Work Act as other employees in Australia, and protections exist for their visa if they call out any breaches to us,” she said.

ABF Commander John Taylor said that migrant workers are essential to South Australia’s viticulture industry and regional economy.

“There’s no place for employers who exploit them – our communities expect better, and so do we.

“The message to employers is simple: do the right thing and meet your obligations.

“Exploiting vulnerable workers or misusing the visa system is unacceptable, and the consequences can be serious,” he said.

Taylor emphasised that Australia “is not a safe haven” for worker exploitation or visa abuse.

Employers looking for free resources on keeping the right records, self-auditing,  labour contracting, and pay information can visit FWO’s agriculture page here which includes a Wine Industry Award section. Employers and employees can also visit fairwork.gov.au, call the FWO Infoline on 13 13 94 or access a free interpreter service by calling 13 14 50.

Employers can also check the work rights of prospective employees by using Home Affairs’ verification system here to ensure they do not unintentionally allow anyone illegal to work.

Are you a Daily Wine News subscriber? If not, click here to join our mailing list. It’s free!

The post Labour law compliance urged as authorities target SA vineyards appeared first on Winetitles.

title: SA wine and tourism future leaders awarded business program scholarship
summary:
10 South Australian professionals have been awarded scholarships under the 2026 Great Wine Capitals Executive Business Program to support them in becoming the next generation of leaders in the wine and tourism sectors. The program provides “valuable upskilling and career development opportunities for early- to mid- career wine and tourism industry professionals”. It will run over six days in May 2026. The SA government said that the initiative is aimed at empowering professionals to strengthen the state’s wine and tourism sectors, and to develop the adaptability, knowledge and resilience to better prepare the individuals for future challenges and opportunities.

The post SA wine and tourism future leaders awarded business program scholarship appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
10 South Australian professionals have been awarded scholarships under the 2026 Great Wine Capitals Executive Business Program to support them in becoming the next generation of leaders in the wine and tourism sectors.

The program provides “valuable upskilling and career development opportunities for early- to mid- career wine and tourism industry professionals”. It will run over six days in May 2026.

The SA government said that the initiative is aimed at empowering professionals to strengthen the state’s wine and tourism sectors, and to develop the adaptability, knowledge and resilience to better prepare the individuals for future challenges and opportunities.

The recipients are as follows:

2026 Great Wine Capitals Executive Business Program scholarship recipients:

Scholarship recipients Business Region
Ben Shaw Home of Plenty Currency Creek
Charlie O’Brien Silent Noise Wines McLaren Vale
Emma Hart Down The Rabbit Hole Wines McLaren Vale
Jessica Nicholls Kimbolton Wines Langhorne Creek
Madison Pike Pikes Wines Clare Valley
Megan Paisley Shaw + Smith Adelaide Hills
Priscila Tamara Diniz Eighteen Fifty & Co Clare Valley
Sally McDonald Zema Estate Coonawarra
Samantha Cartwright Gibson Wines Barossa
Stephen Lindner Turkey Flat Vineyards Barossa

 

The program is a Great Wine Capitals initiative, delivered by Adelaide University and backed by the state government.

“I congratulate the scholarship recipients for this year’s Great Wine Capitals Executive Business Program. I have no doubt that their passion and expertise will help keep South Australia at the forefront of global wine business and tourism.

South Australia produces more than 80 per cent of the nation’s premium wine, from some of the oldest vines in the world,” said Minister for Primary Industries and Regional Development Clare Scriven.

Emily Bourke, Minister for Tourism, Emily Bourke called the program an investment in education and professional development.

“I want to congratulate the scholarship recipients, and I look forward to seeing their expertise and passion for their craft continue to keep South Australia at the forefront of global wine tourism industry.”

Program director of wine and food business at Adelaide University, Dr David Jaud, said that the university is pleased to welcome the recipients to the program for its second year running.

“Building on the success of last year’s cohort, this unique initiative brings together passionate wine industry professionals from across South Australia.

Their expertise and regional perspectives will foster constructive discussion, collaboration, and connections that will grow even further after the program,” Jaud said.

Adelaide University lecturer in marketing and wine business, Dr Irma Dupuis-Day, said that the “comprehensive program facilities learning through subject-matter experts and connections with peers.

“The program will equip participants to accelerate their professional journey and strengthen their contribution to the South Australian wine industry well beyond its completion.”

Are you a Daily Wine News subscriber? If not, click here to join our mailing list. It’s free!

The post SA wine and tourism future leaders awarded business program scholarship appeared first on Winetitles.

title: Yellow Tail records $5.5m loss as US sales falter
summary:
Casella Wines, maker of Yellow Tail, has reported its first loss in 13 years as sales to the US fell by almost 17%, according to The Australian. The $5.5 million loss indicates that Americans may be losing interest in Yellow Tail. The figure is a stark contrast to its profit of $18.6 million in 2024 and $26.5 million in 2023. The company recorded just under $2.1 million in goodwill impairments, a doubling of losses on foreign exchange movements of $15 million, and a $50 million rise in raw materials costs.

The post Yellow Tail records $5.5m loss as US sales falter appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
The wine seems to be losing popularity in the US. Image courtesy Casella Family Brands

Casella Wines, maker of Yellow Tail, has reported its first loss in 13 years as sales to the US fell by almost 17%, according to The Australian. The $5.5 million loss indicates that Americans may be losing interest in Yellow Tail.

The figure is a stark contrast to its profit of $18.6 million in 2024 and $26.5 million in 2023. The company recorded just under $2.1 million in goodwill impairments, a doubling of losses on foreign exchange movements of $15 million, and a $50 million rise in raw materials costs.

Sales for all brands owned by Casella Wines, including Peter Lehmann, Brand’s Laira, Baileys of Glenrowan and Morris of Rutherglen, rose to $546.24 million from $536.58 million. However, actual wine sales from the company fell in 2025, with The Australian crediting the figure increase to Casella’s growing ready-to-drink spirits portfolio and contract brewing.

The latest 2025 accounts lodged with the corporate regulator also reveal “Casella Wines was in breach of its fixed charges cover ratio” 30 June 2025, “although it later received a covenant waiver from its bankers in November 2025”. The winery was able to rollover its banking facility to 30 November 2027.

The company is sitting on borrowings of $190 million, a net asset position of $625.3 million, and total liabilities of slightly under $400 million. The accounts say that it has $62.35 million of cash.

Casella’s Australian sales have outsold America for the first time, sitting at $220.73 million as opposed to the latter’s $172.94 million.

The post Yellow Tail records $5.5m loss as US sales falter appeared first on Winetitles.

title: Taylors Wines appoints new general manager of sales following strategy pivot
summary:
Taylors Wines has today announced the appointment of Mikaela Noonan as general manager of sales for Australia, “marking a significant step in the company’s pivot toward a more focused, premium-led growth strategy”. The winery said that as the Australian wine industry navigates challenging times, it is prioritising premiumisation, reducing complexity, and investing in the long-term health and value of its brands. Noonan joins from Moët Hennesy Australia, where she currently serves as national sales manager – head of independents. Her career experience spans more than 15 years across retail, national accounts, and channel leadership, “underpinned by strong commercial acumen and a deep understanding of both supplier and customer dynamics”.

The post Taylors Wines appoints new general manager of sales following strategy pivot appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
Mikaela Noonan, the new general manager of sales Australia at Taylors Wines. Image supplied

Taylors Wines has today announced the appointment of Mikaela Noonan as general manager of sales for Australia, “marking a significant step in the company’s pivot toward a more focused, premium-led growth strategy”.

The winery said that as the Australian wine industry navigates challenging times, it is prioritising premiumisation, reducing complexity, and investing in the long-term health and value of its brands.

Noonan joins from Moët Hennesy Australia, where she currently serves as national sales manager – head of independents. Her career experience spans more than 15 years across retail, national accounts, and channel leadership, “underpinned by strong commercial acumen and a deep understanding of both supplier and customer dynamics”.

Noonan said that the decision to join Taylors Wines was driven by its clear ambition and the opportunity ahead.

“This is a business with a strong foundation and a clear sense of where it wants to play. The opportunity to help shape a more focused, premium-led growth agenda, while working with a team committed to doing fewer things better, was a compelling one. I’m excited to be part of this next chapter,” she said.

Taylors Wines CEO John Freeman said Noonan’s appointment comes at a pivotal time for the business.

“Mikaela brings strong strategic clarity, commercial discipline, and a deep understanding of the needs and priorities of our customers. She has consistently demonstrated an ability to unlock growth in premium portfolios by focusing on the right opportunities and executing with precision. That is exactly what our business needs as we reset the business for its next phase,” he said.

Freeman emphasised that while industry conditions remain challenging, Taylors Wines is “clear-eyed about where growth will come from”.

“There is no question that the industry is facing pressure. But within that, there are still meaningful pockets of growth and profitability. Our focus is on simplifying where it matters, reducing unnecessary complexity, and backing the strength of our brands where we see the greatest opportunity for sustainable growth.

“We are confident that a more considered and cohesive approach will position us well for the future,” Freeman concluded.

The post Taylors Wines appoints new general manager of sales following strategy pivot appeared first on Winetitles.

title: Hirsch Wine Group launches South American inspired brand featuring ‘lesser-known’ varietals
summary:
The Hirsch Wine Group has launched its new brand of South American inspired ‘lesser-known’ varietals La Chica Con Tina to high praise, with wine critic duo Master of Wine Meg Brodtmann and Mel Gilcrist ranking it in their Top 10 wines of 2025. Translated from Spanish as ‘The Girl With Tattoos’, the brand features two unique varietals – a “vibrant” Arinto and a medium-bodied Garnacha. Hirsch Wine Group said that the wines intend to showcase how Old-World grapes can flourish under Australian skies to produce bold and individual styles. The Arinto and Garnacha grapes are crafted in small batches from “carefully selected parcels” in Heathcote, a region in Victoria known for its ancient soils and generous sunshine

The post Hirsch Wine Group launches South American inspired brand featuring ‘lesser-known’ varietals appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
La Chica Con Tina includes an Arinto and a Garnacha. Image supplied

The Hirsch Wine Group has launched its new brand of South American inspired ‘lesser-known’ varietals La Chica Con Tina to high praise, with wine critic duo Master of Wine Meg Brodtmann and Mel Gilcrist ranking it in their Top 10 wines of 2025.

Translated from Spanish as ‘The Girl With Tattoos’, the brand features two unique varietals – a “vibrant” Arinto and a medium-bodied Garnacha.

Hirsch Wine Group said that the wines intend to showcase how Old-World grapes can flourish under Australian skies to produce bold and individual styles. The Arinto and Garnacha grapes are crafted in small batches from “carefully selected parcels” in Heathcote, a region in Victoria known for its ancient soils and generous sunshine.

The 2025 Arinto is a Heathcote white which aims to capture the sun-drenched spirit of Portugal “reimagined through Australian soil”. Hirsch Wine Group said the wine contains layers of “bright citrus, juicy melon, and ripe stone fruit” intertwined with “hints of delicate florals and a lift of citrus zest” for a crisp taste. The winery said there are also notes of grapefruit and subtle brioche “over a fine line of acidity, finishing with a textured, refreshing edge”.

The 2025 Garnacha is a Heathcote red with a “delicate savoury edge” layered with “fragrant red berries, strawberry and cherry then lifted by hints of clove, orange rind, and spice. Hirsch Wine Group said that the Spanish inspired red is “delightfully different” and should be served chilled.

The project is said to hold personal significance for Hirsch Wine Group CEO Ben Hirsch and chief winemaker Peter Mackey, who both share “a strong affinity for Spanish and Latin American culture”. Hirsch has lived in Spain before, whilst Mackey has worked as a winemaker in Chile, and both speak Spanish.

title: Riverland soars in new Qantas magazine feature campaign
summary:
The Riverland wine region will be spotlighted in back-to-back editions of the Qantas in-flight magazine, positioning the region in front of domestic and international travellers. Through this new promotion, a monthly audience of up to 2.4 million people from across Australia and the world will be introduced to the vast array of premium wines from “a region defined by big skies, bold ideas and the nation’s greatest river at its heart”. The campaign is a part of a partnership between Riverland Wine and Destination Riverland, backed by almost $25,000 from Project 250 – an industry support fund delivered by the South Australian Wine Industry Association and the state government

The post Riverland soars in new Qantas magazine feature campaign appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
The Riverland will be showcased in back-to-back edition of the Qantas in-flight magazine. Image supplied

The Riverland wine region will be spotlighted in back-to-back editions of the Qantas in-flight magazine, positioning the region in front of domestic and international travellers.

Through this new promotion, a monthly audience of up to 2.4 million people from across Australia and the world will be introduced to the vast array of premium wines from “a region defined by big skies, bold ideas and the nation’s greatest river at its heart”.

The campaign is a part of a partnership between Riverland Wine and Destination Riverland, backed by almost $25,000 from Project 250 – an industry support fund delivered by the South Australian Wine Industry Association and the state government.

Minister for Primary Industries and Regional Development, Clare Scriven MLC, said that the government is investing in SA’s winemakers to ensure they can continue to maintain and build the state’s reputation as “the best place in the world to produce, export, and consume premium wine”.

This month’s edition of the Qantas in-flight magazine features a full-page pictorial advertisement showcasing the region’s iconic Murray River and promoting the region’s wine story as a “distinctive Australian wine destination”.

“People may know us for the Murray River, but what they discover here is a dynamic wine community grounded in family, innovation and a deep connection to the land,” said Lauren Ward, general manager for Destination Riverland.

Brigid Nolan, Riverland Wine Chair, said that the campaign is about rebuilding confidence and creating new demand at a time when it is needed the most.

“The Riverland has always been one of Australia’s most important wine-producing regions. By placing our story in front of a national and international audience, we’re actively working to strengthen our regional brand, support visitation, and open pathways into new markets,” Nolan said.

Preparations are already underway for more complementary advertising in the May issue, which will extend the Riverland’s visibility among high-value visitors and wine-curious customers.

The promotion follows a two-page story in the March edition where Qantas content writer Helen Martin independently attested to the Riverland’s landscape, innovation and evolving wine story.

title: Ag leaders demand better Basin management on the Murray River
summary:
The National Farmers’ Federation (NFF), alongside NSW Farmers and Victorian Farmers’ Federation (VFF) stood together last Friday on the banks of the Murray River with a message for the federal government. They demanded that the government deliver smarter Basin management and rule out policies that would strip more water from food production and regional communities “at the worst possible time”. With multiple major water reviews underway and critical decisions imminent, the agriculture leaders said this is the time to reset the approach with one grounded in evidence, practical delivery, and genuine engagement with the people who live and work in the nation’s food sector

The post Ag leaders demand better Basin management on the Murray River appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
The National Farmers’ Federation (NFF), alongside NSW Farmers and Victorian Farmers’ Federation (VFF) stood together last Friday on the banks of the Murray River with a message for the federal government.

They demanded that the government deliver smarter Basin management and rule out policies that would strip more water from food production and regional communities “at the worst possible time”.

With multiple major water reviews underway and critical decisions imminent, the agriculture leaders said this is the time to reset the approach with one grounded in evidence, practical delivery, and genuine engagement with the people who live and work in the nation’s food sector.

The organisations reinforced a strong opposition to water acquisition in any form, including under the 450GL target, Sustainable Diversion Limit adjustments and through rules-based changes that “effectively strip water from productive use”.

NFF president Hamish McIntyre said that the mistakes of the past must not be repeated.

“Communities are still hurting from years of successive buybacks. We’ve seen the damage when policy is done to regions and not with them, and we won’t stand by and let that happen again.

“Farmers are already under pressure from rising input costs and global uncertainty, including the escalating fuel and fertiliser supply issues. If you take away water on top of that, you’re not just hurting farmers, you’re putting Australia’s food and fibre supply at risk,” he said.

McIntyre acknowledged the calls for a Royal Commission into Basin management; however, he urged careful consideration amidst the frustration.

“With multiple substantive reviews already underway and legislative changes on the horizon, the priority right now must be on getting these reforms right. We can’t afford to lose more time or risk unintended consequences.”

The joint delegation drew attention to the draft findings of the Basin Plan Review that recognises the requirement for “smarter water management approaches and projects to improve water quality and address native fish decline, including carp eradication”.

The leaders called for the following:

  • Practical mechanisms that deliver enhanced environmental outcomes and that simply adding more water won’t translate to environmental improvement.
  • A national fish strategy that focuses on real outcomes that reduce fish kills and eradicate carp numbers.
  • A planned approach to repairing or replacing ageing infrastructure, some of which is a century old.
  • Moving on from ‘tinkering’ with rules and water acquisition by stealth.
  • Actively supporting a viable and prosperous agricultural sector that underpins local communities, sustains jobs and safeguards food production for every Australian.

NSW Farmers president Xavier Martin said the government needs to be careful not to cause further water shortages that then create food shortages.

“We want a Basin Plan that supports agricultural productivity, protects regional communities, ensures transparent and accountable Basin governance, and uses science based environmental management rather than blanket water recovery targets.

“The collapsing rivers, the science, and the evidence say we don’t need more buybacks that pour taxpayer money into the ocean, we need projects that use water wisely,” Martin said.

The organisations said that they are actively engaged across four major processes “shaping the future of water policy” including the Basin Plan Review, the Productivity Commission’s Inquiry into National Water Reform, the review of the Water Act 2007, and the review of the Inspector-General of Water Compliance.

The joint representation believes that these processes “must speak to each other” and produce a coherent, workable path forward – “not more uncertainty”.

VFF president Brett Hoskins said that they expect to be listened to.

“We want a Basin Plan that works in the real world. That means smarter management, proper investment, and policies that back in regional communities and avoids destructive buybacks,” he said.

The post Ag leaders demand better Basin management on the Murray River appeared first on Winetitles.

title: Friday Feedback – 10/04
summary:
If you’ve been reading the news and wondering what your peers think of it, look no further. Our Friday Feedback segment provides a platform for the voices of our readers, highlighting the thoughts, reflections and musings of members of the wine industry. See a reader’s response to our last Friday Feedback section and a response to Wine Australia cancelling an export licence last week.

The post Friday Feedback – 10/04 appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
If you’ve been reading the news and wondering what your peers think of it, look no further.

Our Friday Feedback segment provides a platform for the voices of our readers, highlighting the thoughts, reflections and musings of members of the wine industry.

Read our recent roundup here:

 

Responding to Friday Feedback – 27/03

“This EU Trade agreement has a big hidden penalty for all Australians. The “fine print” in this agreement locks Australia into the Paris Agreement and Net Zero. Previously the Paris Agreement and Net Zero was voluntary.

“Also Italian & French wines will be cheaper in Australia, this will hurt particularly our small premium wine producers particularly now when the Australian wine industry is in financial trouble with oversupply, falling consumption and reduced exports.”

  • Ausecco for me too

 

Responding to Wine Australia cancels export licence to ‘protect Australian wine reputation’

“Wine Australia cancels export licence to ‘protect Australian wine reputation’…Protect Australian Wine reputation from what?

“Why was Jindalee’s export license cancelled in the first place?

“Is the current batch of wine CoLab is exporting for Jindalee also breaking the same rules that lost Jindalee’s licence? If Not, what is the problem, is CoLab being targeted by WA for acting as an agent for a banned exporter?

“It’s such a “Nothing, Empty” statement from WA that smacks of high handedness.”

  • CM

 

Read something in Daily Wine News that’s got you thinking? Tell us what you think for the chance to have your comment featured in our Friday Feedback segment. Leave a comment through the button below or contact our editorial team at: journalist@winetitles.com.au.

title: TWE marks milestone in China-Australia immersion program with ceremony and roundtable
summary:
Treasury Wine Estates (TWE) last week completed the Australian leg of the 2026 ‘Australia China Winemaker Immersion Program’. The producer hosted participants, industry partners, and government representatives from both nations at Penfolds Magill Estate for a closing ceremony event and a roundtable discussion on Australia-China relations.

The post TWE marks milestone in China-Australia immersion program with ceremony and roundtable appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
L-R: Kou Yaqin, Zheng Wei, Cai Jieling, and Yang Zhe. The four participants of the immersion program. Image supplied

Treasury Wine Estates (TWE) last week completed the Australian leg of the 2026 ‘Australia China Winemaker Immersion Program’.

The producer hosted participants, industry partners, and government representatives from both nations at Penfolds Magill Estate for a closing ceremony event and a roundtable discussion on Australia-China relations.

The ceremony followed an “intensive” three-week exchange program across South Australia’s wine regions where Chinese winemakers Yang Zhe, Kou Yaqin, and Cai Jieling, along with viticulturist Zheng Wei, explored winemaking and viticulture practices and related academic research, gained hands-on experience in winery and vineyard operations during vintage, and examined cellar‑door and tourism activities in Adelaide.

Speaking at the closing ceremony, TWE chief supply and sustainability officer Kerrin Petty said that the winery is proud to once again deliver the program on behalf of the wine industry associations in Australia and China.

“The participants have been immersed in the very best of South Australia’s food, wine, and tourism culture – hosted by our talented and passionate teams at the most exciting time of year in wine: vintage. We wish them well in their future careers and look forward to seeing how their insight into Australian winemaking practice and research helps to shape the future success of the wine industry,” he said.

The guest of honour, Minister Gao Feng, Minister Counsellor for Economic and Commercial Affairs at the Embassy of the People’s Republic of China in Australia, said it was a pleasure to hear about the broad range of industry experiences they’ve had in South Australia.

“Person-to-person and business-to-business connections are an important foundation for strong relationships between governments and economies, so it’s pleasing to see this in practice by Treasury Wine Estates. Congratulations to all the participants for successfully completing the program,” Feng said.

The program is an “industry-first exchange” designed to nurture talent and deepen cultural understanding through immersion into the winemaking practices and market dynamics of both countries. Now in its second year, the program is supported by Australian Grape & Wine (AGW) and the China Alcoholic Drinks Association (CADA).

“The return of the Australia–China Winemaker Immersion Program for a second year demonstrates the value of industry-led collaboration in building capability, trust and long-term relationships. This program is leading the way in strengthening the professional and cultural links that underpin a resilient and sustainable wine sector,” said AGW CEO Lee McLean.

title: Calabria Family Wine Group takes on national distribution of Redman
summary:
Coonawarra winery Redman has revealed that The Calabria Family Wine Group will be its new national distributor as of Monday 13 April 2026. The partnership will see Redman wines return to Western Australia after a 15-year hiatus. Calabria’s acquisition of The Wine Company, who previously distributed Redman across New South Wales, Victoria and Queensland, led to the change.

The post Calabria Family Wine Group takes on national distribution of Redman appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
Calabria will distribute Redman nationally following its acquisition of The Wine Company. Image supplied

Coonawarra winery Redman has revealed that The Calabria Family Wine Group will be its new national distributor as of Monday 13 April 2026.

The partnership will see Redman wines return to Western Australia after a 15-year hiatus.

Calabria’s acquisition of The Wine Company, who previously distributed Redman across New South Wales, Victoria and Queensland, led to the change.

The move has been welcomed by Redman co-owner and fourth-generation winemaker Dan Redman.

“Calabria is a multi-generational, family-owned business like ours, and we are confident in their understanding of our business. We are excited to be a part of their growing distribution company.

“We look forward to continuing to nurture our existing customer relationships, as well as creating many more with Calabria as our new partner,” he said.

Andrew Calabria, head of sales and marketing at Calabria Family Wine Group said that Calabria is very proud to be working with one of Coonawarra’s longest standing family producers.

“The Redman family is at the heart of Australian winemaking, we are excited to represent Redman and look forward to sharing their story and milestones.”

2026 is the 60th year of the Redman brand.

title: Labour law compliance urged as authorities target SA vineyards
summary:
The Australian Taxation Office (ATO), Fair Work Ombudsman (FWO) and Department of Home Affairs’ Australian Border Force (ABF) have conducted joint coordinated unannounced inspections targeting vineyard owners and labour hire providers. Surprise compliance visits to 18 vineyards in the Barossa Valley, Adelaide Hills and McLaren Vale have already been carried out as a part of ‘Operation Zephyr’ under the Shadow Economy Taskforce. Investigators were searching for any unlawful employer practices regarding seasonal vineyard workers, like labour hire providers, including:

The post Labour law compliance urged as authorities target SA vineyards appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
Coordinated unannounced inspections targeted SA vineyard owners and labour hirers. Image supplied

The Australian Taxation Office (ATO), Fair Work Ombudsman (FWO) and Department of Home Affairs’ Australian Border Force (ABF) have conducted joint coordinated unannounced inspections targeting vineyard owners and labour hire providers.

Surprise compliance visits to 18 vineyards in the Barossa Valley, Adelaide Hills and McLaren Vale have already been carried out as a part of ‘Operation Zephyr’ under the Shadow Economy Taskforce.

Officers from all three agencies spoke directly with workers, vineyard managers and labour hire providers and requested detailed records including any labour hire contracts, invoices, timesheets, pay slips and work rosters.

Authorities have indicated that investigations are ongoing.

Australian Grape & Wine (AGW) CEO Lee McLean said that the organisation is aware of Operation Zephyr from media reports.

“We take these allegations very seriously. The Australian wine sector is built on integrity, and compliance with workplace, taxation and migration laws is fundamental to maintaining trust across the industry.

“We note that this is an ongoing investigation and, as reported, no arrests have been made.

“Australian Grape & Wine supports appropriate enforcement action where wrongdoing is identified. Unlawful labour practices and exploitation have no place in our industry and undermine both workers and businesses doing the right thing,” he said.

Inca Lee, chief executive of the South Australian Wine Industry Association (SAWIA), said that it is also aware of the enforcement activity. She said that the association has provided information to its members.

“The South Australian grape and wine industry is committed to operating with integrity and in full compliance with all relevant legislation. Our grape and wine businesses contribute positively to the state’s economy and regional communities.

“SAWIA continues to support members through expert guidance and practical resources to ensure they understand and meet their obligations, and we will continue to work closely with government agencies to maintain high standards across the industry,” she said.

Investigators were searching for “any unlawful practices” by employers of seasonal vineyard workers and labour hire providers, including:

  • underpaying wages, penalty rates and other entitlements including super
  • non-compliance with tax, super and registry obligations
  • failing to withhold and remit Pay as You Go Withholding (PAYGW) tax
  • failing to provide pay slips to workers
  • non-lodgement of activity statements and income tax returns
  • incorrect reporting of business income and expenses
  • breaches of obligations under the Migration Act including migrant worker exploitation and unlawful provision of immigration assistance.

ATO Assistant Commissioner Tony Goding said that unlawful labour practices can cause significant disadvantage to honest operators in the viticulture industry.

“Paying workers off the books, ignoring ATO obligations or using dodgy labour hire providers doesn’t just break the law, it erodes trust in the viticulture industry which employs thousands of people across Australia.

“Our work in South Australia’s grape-growing regions shows we are serious about crushing illegal labour hire practices to ensure a fair go for honest providers,” he said.

Fair Work Ombudsman Anna Booth said that the pay and conditions of the employees on the ground in South Australia’s wine regions need to meet the same high standards as the impressive produce.

“The agriculture sector is a priority for the Fair Work Ombudsman and viticulture is an important part of that focus. We know there are a large number of vulnerable workers employed in grape-picking, packing, and pruning vineyards. Not paying workers their entitlements cannot be part of any grower’s business model.

“Migrant workers must remember that they have the same workplace rights under the Fair Work Act as other employees in Australia, and protections exist for their visa if they call out any breaches to us,” she said.

ABF Commander John Taylor said that migrant workers are essential to South Australia’s viticulture industry and regional economy.

“There’s no place for employers who exploit them – our communities expect better, and so do we.

“The message to employers is simple: do the right thing and meet your obligations.

“Exploiting vulnerable workers or misusing the visa system is unacceptable, and the consequences can be serious,” he said.

Taylor emphasised that Australia “is not a safe haven” for worker exploitation or visa abuse.

Employers looking for free resources on keeping the right records, self-auditing,  labour contracting, and pay information can visit FWO’s agriculture page here which includes a Wine Industry Award section. Employers and employees can also visit fairwork.gov.au, call the FWO Infoline on 13 13 94 or access a free interpreter service by calling 13 14 50.

Employers can also check the work rights of prospective employees by using Home Affairs’ verification system here to ensure they do not unintentionally allow anyone illegal to work.

The post Labour law compliance urged as authorities target SA vineyards appeared first on Winetitles.

title: SA wine and tourism future leaders awarded business program scholarship
summary:
10 South Australian professionals have been awarded scholarships under the 2026 Great Wine Capitals Executive Business Program to support them in becoming the next generation of leaders in the wine and tourism sectors. The program provides “valuable upskilling and career development opportunities for early- to mid- career wine and tourism industry professionals”. It will run over six days in May 2026. The SA government said that the initiative is aimed at empowering professionals to strengthen the state’s wine and tourism sectors, and to develop the adaptability, knowledge and resilience to better prepare the individuals for future challenges and opportunities.

The post SA wine and tourism future leaders awarded business program scholarship appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
10 South Australian professionals have been awarded scholarships under the 2026 Great Wine Capitals Executive Business Program to support them in becoming the next generation of leaders in the wine and tourism sectors.

The program provides “valuable upskilling and career development opportunities for early- to mid- career wine and tourism industry professionals”. It will run over six days in May 2026.

The SA government said that the initiative is aimed at empowering professionals to strengthen the state’s wine and tourism sectors, and to develop the adaptability, knowledge and resilience to better prepare the individuals for future challenges and opportunities.

The recipients are as follows:

2026 Great Wine Capitals Executive Business Program scholarship recipients:

Scholarship recipients Business Region
Ben Shaw Home of Plenty Currency Creek
Charlie O’Brien Silent Noise Wines McLaren Vale
Emma Hart Down The Rabbit Hole Wines McLaren Vale
Jessica Nicholls Kimbolton Wines Langhorne Creek
Madison Pike Pikes Wines Clare Valley
Megan Paisley Shaw + Smith Adelaide Hills
Priscila Tamara Diniz Eighteen Fifty & Co Clare Valley
Sally McDonald Zema Estate Coonawarra
Samantha Cartwright Gibson Wines Barossa
Stephen Lindner Turkey Flat Vineyards Barossa

 

The program is a Great Wine Capitals initiative, delivered by Adelaide University and backed by the state government.

“I congratulate the scholarship recipients for this year’s Great Wine Capitals Executive Business Program. I have no doubt that their passion and expertise will help keep South Australia at the forefront of global wine business and tourism.

South Australia produces more than 80 per cent of the nation’s premium wine, from some of the oldest vines in the world,” said Minister for Primary Industries and Regional Development Clare Scriven.

Emily Bourke, Minister for Tourism, Emily Bourke called the program an investment in education and professional development.

“I want to congratulate the scholarship recipients, and I look forward to seeing their expertise and passion for their craft continue to keep South Australia at the forefront of global wine tourism industry.”

Program director of wine and food business at Adelaide University, Dr David Jaud, said that the university is pleased to welcome the recipients to the program for its second year running.

“Building on the success of last year’s cohort, this unique initiative brings together passionate wine industry professionals from across South Australia.

Their expertise and regional perspectives will foster constructive discussion, collaboration, and connections that will grow even further after the program,” Jaud said.

Adelaide University lecturer in marketing and wine business, Dr Irma Dupuis-Day, said that the “comprehensive program facilities learning through subject-matter experts and connections with peers.

“The program will equip participants to accelerate their professional journey and strengthen their contribution to the South Australian wine industry well beyond its completion.”

title: Yellow Tail records $5.5m loss as US sales falter
summary:
Casella Wines, maker of Yellow Tail, has reported its first loss in 13 years as sales to the US fell by almost 17%, according to The Australian. The $5.5 million loss indicates that Americans may be losing interest in Yellow Tail. The figure is a stark contrast to its profit of $18.6 million in 2024 and $26.5 million in 2023. The company recorded just under $2.1 million in goodwill impairments, a doubling of losses on foreign exchange movements of $15 million, and a $50 million rise in raw materials costs.

The post Yellow Tail records $5.5m loss as US sales falter appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
The wine seems to be losing popularity in the US. Image courtesy Casella Family Brands

Casella Wines, maker of Yellow Tail, has reported its first loss in 13 years as sales to the US fell by almost 17%, according to The Australian. The $5.5 million loss indicates that Americans may be losing interest in Yellow Tail.

The figure is a stark contrast to its profit of $18.6 million in 2024 and $26.5 million in 2023. The company recorded just under $2.1 million in goodwill impairments, a doubling of losses on foreign exchange movements of $15 million, and a $50 million rise in raw materials costs.

Sales for all brands owned by Casella Wines, including Peter Lehmann, Brand’s Laira, Baileys of Glenrowan and Morris of Rutherglen, rose to $546.24 million from $536.58 million. However, actual wine sales from the company fell in 2025, with The Australian crediting the figure increase to Casella’s growing ready-to-drink spirits portfolio and contract brewing.

The latest 2025 accounts lodged with the corporate regulator also reveal “Casella Wines was in breach of its fixed charges cover ratio” 30 June 2025, “although it later received a covenant waiver from its bankers in November 2025”. The winery was able to rollover its banking facility to 30 November 2027.

The company is sitting on borrowings of $190 million, a net asset position of $625.3 million, and total liabilities of slightly under $400 million. The accounts say that it has $62.35 million of cash.

Casella’s Australian sales have outsold America for the first time, sitting at $220.73 million as opposed to the latter’s $172.94 million.

The post Yellow Tail records $5.5m loss as US sales falter appeared first on Winetitles.
